# Climate crisis caused half of European heat deaths in 2022, says study

Researchers found 38,000 fewer people – 10 times number of murders – would have died if atmosphere was not clogged with greenhouse pollutants

Climate breakdown caused more than half of the 68,000 heat deaths during the scorching European summer of 2022, a [study](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41612-024-00783-2) has found.

Researchers from the Barcelona Institute for Global Health (ISGlobal) found 38,000 fewer people would have died from heat if humans had not clogged the atmosphere with pollutants that act like a greenhouse and bake the planet. The death toll is about 10 times greater than the number of people murdered in [Europe](https://www.theguardian.com/world/europe-news) that year.

“Many see climate change as a future concern,” said the lead author, Thessa Beck. “Yet our findings underscore that it is already a pressing issue.”

The warm weather killed more women than men, more southern Europeans than northern Europeans, and more older people than younger people. Scientists already knew carbon pollution had made the heatwaves hotter but did not know how much it had driven up the death toll.

They found 56% of the heat-related deaths could have been avoided if the world had not been warmed by burning fossil fuels and the destruction of nature. The share varied between 44% and 54% in the six years prior.

Even small increases in temperatures can have devastating impacts on public health, said Emily Theokritoff, a researcher at Imperial College London who was not involved in the study. “This result makes sense – heat-related death increases rapidly as temperatures push past the limits people are acclimatised to.”

Europe is warming twice as fast as the rest of the planet but doctors warn its hospitals are not prepared to deal with the consequences. The rise in temperatures forces more people to endure searing summer heat that pushes their bodies into overdrive even as it cuts exposure to chilling winter cold that leaves them too weak to fight off illness.

Scientists [project](https://www.theguardian.com/society/article/2024/aug/21/heat-deaths-in-europe-may-triple-by-end-of-the-century-study-finds) the lives lost to hotter summers in Europe will outstrip those saved by cooler winters if the planet heats more than 2C above preindustrial levels. Last week, the UN environment programme warned the world is on track to heat by 3C by the end of the century.

The dangers of extreme heat are even greater in Africa, Asia, and South America, but a lack of data had limited studies on how it affects human health, said Beck.

“A common misconception is that only extreme temperatures pose a serious risk,” she said. “However, our study, along with previous research, shows that even moderate heat can lead to heat-related deaths, particularly among more vulnerable populations.”

Scientists had previously used heat and health data for 35 European countries to estimate how many more people die as a result of hot weather. In the new study, they ran the model with temperatures for a hypothetical world in which humans had not heated the planet.

They found climate change was behind 22,501 heat deaths in women and 14,026 heat deaths in men.

Garyfallos Konstantinoudis, a researcher at Imperial College London, who was not involved in the study, said the authors may have overestimated the effect of heat on mortality because they did not account for how people had adapted.

He said: “Previous studies have reported a decrease in heat-mortality impact over time, due to factors including infrastructural changes and improved health care.”

To stay safe in the heat, doctors recommend drinking water, staying indoors during the hottest parts of the day and looking after older neighbours and relatives who live alone. Governments can save lives by creating action plans for hot weather, designing cities with more green space and less concrete, and cutting pollution.

“Heat can be very dangerous for the heart, especially for older people,” said Beck.

# ESOTC 2022 | EUROPE | IN FOCUS Extreme heat

## ESOTC Menu

The year as a whole was very warm across Europe. This is reflected in the average temperature, which ranked second warmest, after 2020, since the beginning of the 20th century. For more details on the long-term records, see the ‘[Temperature](https://climate.copernicus.eu/climate-indicators/temperature)’ Climate Indicator.

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig1.png)

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig1.png)

*Figure 1. Ranking of the average 2022 daily temperature for the period from 1950 to 2022. Darker shades of red indicate a higher ranking. Data source: E-OBS. Credit: KNMI/C3S/ECMWF.*  
[HIGH RESOLUTION IMAGE](https://climate.copernicus.eu/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig1.pdf)

For most of southwestern Europe, the average daily temperature of 2022 was the highest recorded since 1950. Periods with unseasonably warm conditions occurred throughout the year, in some cases reaching extreme highs. The year was characterised by more warm episodes than cold spells: Europe was much colder than the average for the 1991–2020 reference period only during one brief period in April, while there were six pan-European warm spells. More about this can be read in the ‘[Temperature](https://climate.copernicus.eu/esotc/2022/temperature)’ section.

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig2.png)

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig2.png)

*Figure 2. ‘Warm daytimes’ anomaly (%) for 2022, relative to the average for the 1991–2020 reference period. Data source: E-OBS. Credit: KNMI/C3S/ECMWF.*   
[HIGH RESOLUTION IMAGE](https://climate.copernicus.eu/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig2.pdf)

Most of Europe experienced a larger number of ‘warm daytimes’ in 2022 than average, with up to 30% more observed in southwestern and western areas. This illustrates the high frequency of temperature extremes during the year.

The most anomalous periods of warm spells were recorded in March, May, during summer and autumn as a whole, and in December. The May and summer warm spells can be referred to as heatwaves, as the absolute temperatures during these periods were very high. More information on the annual, seasonal and daily anomalies throughout the year can be found in the ‘[Temperature](https://climate.copernicus.eu/esotc/2022/temperature)’ section.

Below, the periods of exceptional warmth are documented and quantified. The role of atmospheric circulation is highlighted and some of the potential impacts of the extremely high summer temperatures on human health are included.

## High and extreme temperatures in 2022

### March warm spell

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig3a.png)

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig3a.png)

*Figure 3a. Number of ‘warm daytimes’ anomaly (%) in March 2022, relative to the average for the 1991–2020 reference period (shades of red to blue) and monthly average mean sea level pressure (contours). Data source: E-OBS. Credit: KNMI/C3S/ECMWF.*  
[HIGH RESOLUTION IMAGE](https://climate.copernicus.eu/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig3a.pdf)

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig3b.png)

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig3b.png)

*Figure 3b. Warmest daytime temperature anomaly (°C) in March 2022, relative to the average for the 1991–2020 reference period. Data source: E-OBS. Credit: KNMI/C3S/ECMWF.*  
[HIGH RESOLUTION IMAGE](https://climate.copernicus.eu/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig3b.pdf)

In northern Europe, there were up to 50% more warm daytimes than average during March and daily maximum temperatures for the month were as much as 8°C higher than average. A pronounced anticyclone centred over the Baltic Sea drew warm air from further south to northwestern and northern Europe.

Meanwhile, cold air was transported southward, leading to fewer warm daytimes over the Iberian Peninsula, southern France, Italy and the Balkans. Over Europe as a whole, March saw the largest positive anomalies of sunshine hours on record. This is discussed further in the ‘[Clouds and sunshine duration](https://climate.copernicus.eu/esotc/2022/clouds-and-sunshine-duration)’ and ‘[Energy](https://climate.copernicus.eu/esotc/2022/energy)’ sections.

### May heatwave

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig4a.png)

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig4a.png)

*Figure 4a. Number of ‘warm daytimes’ anomaly (%) for May 2022, relative to the average for the 1991–2020 reference period (shades of red to blue) and monthly average of the mean sea level pressure (contours). Data source: E-OBS. Credit: KNMI/C3S/ECMWF.*  
[HIGH RESOLUTION IMAGE](https://climate.copernicus.eu/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig4a.pdf)

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig4b.png)

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig4b.png)

*Figure 4b. Warmest daytime temperature anomaly (°C) for May 2022, relative to the average for the 1991–2020 reference period. Data source: E-OBS. Credit: KNMI/C3S/ECMWF.*  
[HIGH RESOLUTION IMAGE](https://climate.copernicus.eu/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig4b.pdf)

In May, southern Europe, in particular Portugal, Spain and France, saw a higher number of warm daytimes than average. Conversely, fewer warm days were experienced over northern and northeastern Europe. Several locations along the northern coast of the Mediterranean Sea and over the Alps experienced a much higher percentage – up to 40% – of warm daytimes. The atmospheric circulation contributed to the high number of warm daytimes; a dominant anticyclone with its centre over the Bay of Biscay favoured cloud-free conditions and a general downward motion in the atmosphere. The slow sinking of the air mass compressed and warmed the atmosphere.

May 2022 saw comparable positive anomalies of [sunshine hours](/esotc/2022/clouds-and-sunshine-duration#sunshine) to March.

The warm conditions of May 2022 resulted in extreme temperatures; the hottest day of the month saw values up to 8°C warmer than average in some parts of Spain and France.

### Summer heatwave

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig5a.png)

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig5a.png)

*Figure 5a. ‘**Warm daytimes*'*anomaly (%) for summer (June–August) 2022, relative to the average for the 1991–2020 reference period. Data source: E-OBS. Credit: KNMI/C3S/ECMWF.*  
[HIGH RESOLUTION IMAGE](https://climate.copernicus.eu/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig5a.pdf)

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig5b.png)

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig5b.png)

*Figure 5b. Warmest daytime temperature anomaly for summer (June–August) 2022, relative to the average for the 1991–2020 reference period. Data source: E-OBS. Credit: KNMI/C3S/ECMWF.*  
[HIGH RESOLUTION IMAGE](https://climate.copernicus.eu/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig5b.pdf)

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig5c.png)

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig5c.png)

*Figure 5c. Average daily maximum temperature anomaly for summer (June–August) 2022, relative to the average for the 1991–2020 reference period. Data source: E-OBS. Credit: KNMI/C3S/ECMWF.*  
[HIGH RESOLUTION IMAGE](https://climate.copernicus.eu/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig5c.pdf)

The heatwave in May was followed by the warmest summer on record for Europe. The number of warm daytimes was exceptionally high, especially in southwestern Europe. The highest temperatures reached were in western Europe, at about 10°C higher than typical summer maximum temperatures.

Characteristic of the hot summer was the early arrival of the first tropical day of the year. In some parts of Europe, including central Europe and much of France, the [first tropical day arrived around mid-May](https://climate.copernicus.eu/seasonal-review-europes-record-breaking-summer) which is about a month earlier than usual.

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/FIGURE_6_ESOTC_EXTREME_HEAT.png)

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/FIGURE_6_ESOTC_EXTREME_HEAT.png)

Figure 6. Average mean sea level pressure for June (left) and July (right) for the 1991–2020 reference period (lines) and related anomalies for June and July 2022 (shades of red to blue). Data source: ERA5. Credit: C3S/ECMWF/DWD  
[HIGH RESOLUTION IMAGE](https://climate.copernicus.eu/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/FIGURE_6_ESOTC_EXTREME_HEAT.pdf)

In June 2022, temperatures were above average across most of Europe, with southern parts of the continent, from the Iberian Peninsula across France and into Italy, most affected. Despite numerous local record-breaking events, the monthly average temperature for Europe was only the second warmest on record, at 0.3°C cooler than [June 2019](https://climate.copernicus.eu/ESOTC/2019/european-temperature).

In July, an area of [high pressure settled over western Europe](https://climate.copernicus.eu/esotc/2022/atmospheric-circulation), bringing dry and sunny conditions. Combined with hot air flow from North Africa, this led to heatwave conditions over most of western Europe. As a result, temperatures soared to above 40°C for at least one day in [Spain, France](https://climate.copernicus.eu/europe-continued-swelter-july) and, for the first time ever recorded, in the United Kingdom[[1]](#). Globally, increasingly high latitudes are seeing more extreme temperatures, including those exceeding 40°C.

In August, temperatures were higher than average across most of Europe. They were most above average in the far east of the continent and generally high in the west, but were not as extreme as experienced earlier in the summer.

The frequent occurrence of extreme temperatures, accompanied by persistent lack of precipitation, was closely linked with the concurrent, widespread [drought](https://climate.copernicus.eu/esotc/2022/drought) observed in most of Europe throughout the summer.

### October warm spell

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig7a.png)

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig7a.png)

*Figure 7a. Anomalies in the number of ‘warm daytimes’ (%) and in sea surface temperature (°C) for October 2022, relative to the averages for the 1991–2020 reference period (shades of red to blue), and average mean sea level pressure for October 2022 (contours). Data source: ERA5/E-OBS/C3S SST v2.1 dataset. Credit:C3S/ECMWF/KNMI/UK Met Office.*  
[HIGH RESOLUTION IMAGE](https://climate.copernicus.eu/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig7a.pdf)

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig7b.png)

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig7b.png)

*Figure 7b. Average daily maximum temperature (°C) for October 2022, relative to the average for the 1991–2020 reference period. Data source: ERA5 and E-OBS. Credit: KNMI/C3S/ECMWF.*  
[HIGH RESOLUTION IMAGE](https://climate.copernicus.eu/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig7b.pdf)

In October, an unusually high number of warm daytimes occurred over most parts of Europe. The highest numbers were in southern and western Europe, reaching as much as 30-40% higher than average in some locations.

This was partly due to a pronounced cyclone, with its centre located south of Iceland, bringing air from a warmer-than-average Atlantic Ocean and southwestern Europe into the central part of the continent.

For Europe as a whole, it was the [warmest October on record](https://climate.copernicus.eu/esotc/2022/temperature), but the highest average monthly temperatures were found in a band from eastern Spain, reaching across France and into central Europe. In these regions, the average daily maximum temperature for the month was up to 7°C higher than typical daily maximum temperatures during October.

### December warm spell

After a [cold start to the month, December 2022](https://climate.copernicus.eu/esotc/2022/temperature) became exceptionally warm at the very end of the year.

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig8.png)

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig8.png)

*Figure 8. Warmest daytime temperature anomaly (°C) for December 2022, relative to the average for the 1991–2020 reference period. Data source: E-OBS. Credit: KNMI/C3S/ECMWF.*  
[HIGH RESOLUTION IMAGE](https://climate.copernicus.eu/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig8.pdf)

For most of Europe, the warmest day in December 2022 was much warmer than average, except for much of Scandinavia and the northeastern areas of the continent. Central Europe experienced exceptional conditions, with the warmest day up to 8°C warmer than average.

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig9.png)

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig9.png)

*Figure 9. Average daily maximum temperature (°C) for central Europe, from 1 December 2022 to 7 January 2023 (red), and the 10th and 90th percentiles of the daily maximum temperatures during the 1991–2020 reference period (grey band). The blue line gives the median daily maximum temperature. Data source: E-OBS. Credit: KNMI/C3S/ECMWF.*  
[HIGH RESOLUTION IMAGE](https://climate.copernicus.eu/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/Rebranded%20Figures/C3S_ESOTC2022_in_focus_heat_fig9.pdf)

For central Europe, this warm spell occurred in the latter half of December 2022. Temperatures quickly exceeded the 90th percentile, reaching nearly 14°C on 31 December and 1 January; 10°C above the median value for these days.

## Impacts of high summer temperatures on health

Heat stress indices represent the effect of the environment on the human body. One such index is the Universal Thermal Climate Index (UTCI), which takes into account temperature, humidity, wind speed, sunshine and heat emitted by the surroundings, and how the human body responds to different thermal environments. It can be used to infer weather-induced outdoor thermal stress in humans by classifying UTCI values into ten different heat and cold stress categories, with units of oC representing a ‘feels-like’ temperature. For summer, the focus is on maximum (daytime) daily UTCI.

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/FIGURE_10A_ESOTC_EXTREME_HEAT.png)

*Figure 10a. Number of days that experienced ‘strong heat stress’ (UTCI between 32 and 38°C) during June, July and August 2022. Data source: ERA5-HEAT. Credit: C3S/ECMWF.*  
[HIGH RESOLUTION IMAGE](https://climate.copernicus.eu/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/FIGURE_10A_ESOTC_EXTREME_HEAT.pdf)

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/FIGURE_10B_ESOTC_EXTREME_HEAT.png)

*Figure 10b. Number of days that experienced ‘very strong heat stress’ (UTCI between 38 and 46°C) during June, July and August 2022. Data source: ERA5-HEAT. Credit: C3S/ECMWF.*  
[HIGH RESOLUTION IMAGE](https://climate.copernicus.eu/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/FIGURE_10B_ESOTC_EXTREME_HEAT.pdf)

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/FIGURE_10C_ESOTC_EXTREME_HEAT.png)

*Figure 10c. Number of days that experienced ‘extreme heat stress’ (UTCI above 46°C) during June, July and August 2022. Data source: ERA5-HEAT. Credit: C3S/ECMWF.*  
[HIGH RESOLUTION IMAGE](https://climate.copernicus.eu/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/FIGURE_10C_ESOTC_EXTREME_HEAT.pdf)

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/FIGURE_10D_ESOTC_EXTREME_HEAT.png)

*Figure 10d. Number of days that experienced ‘no heat stress’ (UTCI between 9 and 26°C) during June, July and August 2022. Data source: ERA5-HEAT. Credit: C3S/ECMWF.*   
[HIGH RESOLUTION IMAGE](https://climate.copernicus.eu/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/FIGURE_10D_ESOTC_EXTREME_HEAT.pdf)

During summer 2022, much of Europe saw large heat stress anomalies, with some areas, such as the United Kingdom and Germany, exceeding typical July maximum UTCI values by up to 18oC on 19 July. Large parts of southern and western Europe experienced more than five days of ‘very strong heat stress’ during the summer, with several regions in the southwest experiencing upwards of 50 days in this category. Also in the southwest, small areas experienced up to 10 days of ‘extreme heat stress’, for which immediate action is required to avoid heat stroke[[2]](#).

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/FIGURE_11A_ESOTC_EXTREME_HEAT.png)

*Figure 11a. Percentage of days during summer with ‘no heat stress’ (UTCI between 9 and 26°C, top) and ‘very strong heat stress’ (UTCI between 38 and 46°C, bottom) in southern Europe, from 1950 to 2022. Data source: ERA5-HEAT. Credit C3S/ECMWF.*  
[HIGH RESOLUTION IMAGE](https://climate.copernicus.eu/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/FIGURE_11A_ESOTC_EXTREME_HEAT.pdf)

 

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/FIGURE_11B_ESOTC_EXTREME_HEAT.png)

*Figure 11b. Percentage of days during summer with ‘no heat stress’ (UTCI between 9 and 26°C, top) and ‘very strong heat stress’ (UTCI between 38 and 46°C, bottom) in northern Europe, from 1950 to 2022. Data source: ERA5-HEAT. Credit C3S/ECMWF.*  
[HIGH RESOLUTION IMAGE](https://climate.copernicus.eu/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/FIGURE_11B_ESOTC_EXTREME_HEAT.pdf)

Across Europe, there is an upward trend in the number of days in summer during which the maximum UTCI falls within the categories of ‘strong’ and ‘very strong heat stress’. In southern Europe, there is also an increase in the number of days with ‘extreme heat stress’. This occurs alongside a decrease in the number of days experiencing ‘no’ or ‘moderate heat stress’. In southern Europe, summer 2022 had the highest number of days with ‘very strong heat stress’ in a record that goes back to 1950, and the second lowest number of days with ‘no heat stress’. In northern Europe, from the 1950s to 1990s, the number of days with ‘no heat stress’ during summer was more consistent, with around a third of the season seeing ‘no heat stress’ on average. However, there is a decreasing trend and in 2022 the number of such days reduced to just 24%, the second lowest percentage on record.

![](/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/FIGURE_12_ESOTC_EXTREME_HEAT.png)

*Figure 12. Forecast of daily maximum 8-hour average surface ozone concentration on 19 July 2022. Data source: CAMS regional model ensemble analysis. Credit: CAMS/ECMWF.*  
[HIGH RESOLUTION IMAGE](https://climate.copernicus.eu/sites/default/files/custom-uploads/ESOTC2022/europe/extreme_heat/FIGURE_12_ESOTC_EXTREME_HEAT.pdf)

Periods of exceptionally high temperatures and long sunshine hours lead to the efficient production of ozone from precursor pollutants, all primarily generated during the combustion of fossil fuels. High levels of surface ozone pollution are common across Europe and the Mediterranean regions in summer. In summer 2022, large parts of northwestern Europe experienced conditions, including high temperatures, high surface pressure (Figure 6) and above-average sunshine hours, in combination with below-average winds, that favoured the accumulation of precursors in the lower atmosphere and thus the build-up of ozone levels. Peak surface ozone concentrations occurred across much of Europe from 18-20 July, with values in excess of the EU-recommended[[3]](#) 24-hour threshold set for the protection of human health, of 120 μg/m3, and peaking at above 200 μg/m3 in regions of the western North Sea. The ozone distribution showed a similar spatial pattern to the UTCI, posing additional health risks to vulnerable people and those with respiratory conditions in areas experiencing ‘strong’ to ‘very strong heat stress’.

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/SurfaceAirTemperatureAnomalyJuly2022.jpg/250px-SurfaceAirTemperatureAnomalyJuly2022.jpg)

Between June and September 2022, large parts of [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") were affected by persistent [heatwaves](/wiki/Heatwave "Heatwave") which killed tens of thousands and caused billions of euros in damage. They were the deadliest meteorological event of the year and caused [thousands of wildfires](/wiki/2022_European_and_Mediterranean_wildfires "2022 European and Mediterranean wildfires"), as well as [widespread droughts](/wiki/2022_European_drought "2022 European drought") across much of the continent.

The first heatwave, which came in June, led to temperatures of 40–43 °C (104–109 °F), with most severe temperature anomalies in France, where several records were broken. A second more severe heatwave occurred in mid-July, [extending north to the United Kingdom](/wiki/2022_United_Kingdom_heatwaves "2022 United Kingdom heatwaves"), where temperatures surpassing 40 °C (104 °F) were recorded for the first time. A third heatwave began in August, with parts of France and Spain expected to reach temperatures as high as 38 °C (100 °F). Although temperatures in most places subsided in August, a smaller heatwave impacted France on 12 September, with temperatures reaching 40 °C (104 °F) once again.

In late 2023, a study published by the [Barcelona Institute for Global Health](/w/index.php?title=Barcelona_Institute_for_Global_Health&action=edit&redlink=1 "Barcelona Institute for Global Health (page does not exist)") estimated that there were over 70,000 heat-related deaths across Europe during the heatwave, a significant increase from earlier estimates.

## Meteorology

The June heatwave was the result of an interaction among the high pressures that generate atmospheric stability; [Tropical Storm Alex](/wiki/Tropical_Storm_Alex_(2022) "Tropical Storm Alex (2022)"); the strong sunshine of the boreal summer; and an [air mass](/wiki/Air_mass "Air mass") emanating from North Africa that had entered the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") loaded with suspended dust that caused [haze](/wiki/Haze "Haze") in the centre and south of the peninsula.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Climatologists linked the extreme heat to the impact of [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"), and experts predict that changes in the [jet stream](/wiki/Jet_stream "Jet stream") as a result of climate change will cause heatwaves with increasing frequency [in Europe](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Europe "Climate change in Europe").[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) Furthermore, due to the jet stream, the increase in heatwaves for European countries is three-to-four times higher than other countries in northern mid-latitudes, such as the United States.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Reports of the death toll varied significantly. In July 2023, researchers from Barcelona Institute for Global Health estimated 61,672 heat-induced problems between 30 May and 4 September in a finding published in *[Nature Medical](/wiki/Nature_(journal) "Nature (journal)")*.[[23]](#cite_note-D61K-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) The study gathered data from 35 countries with a combined population of 543 million people. A statistical framework was paired against historical meteorological records to observe the trend of deaths. The researchers said that they used weekly mortality data to develop their model which may not allow for short-term changes and hence may underestimate the actual number of deaths. Countries around the Mediterranean Sea, especially Italy, Spain, Portugal and Greece, experienced higher death rates.[[25]](#cite_note-25) In November 2022, Reuters stated that there were 20,000 "excess" deaths recorded; deaths which officials did not directly attribute to heat but may be heat-induced.[[26]](#cite_note-Portala1-26) In November 2023, the Barcelona Institute for Global Health revised their number to over 70,000 "excess" deaths after developing a new method to calculate the mortality rate. The new research utilised daily temperature and mortality data as opposed to the previous research method using weekly data. The researchers claimed the previous figure underestimated the true number by 10.28 percent.[[27]](#cite_note-Chadwick1-27)

## By country

### Andorra

On 15 June, Andorra's weather agency, the [National Meteorological Service of Andorra](/w/index.php?title=National_Meteorological_Service&action=edit&redlink=1 "National Meteorological Service (page does not exist)"), issued a "significant danger weather warning" due to heat. The weather warning was expected to last until 27 July with 24 and 25 July being the days in which the agency expected to raise the warning to "extreme danger weather warning".[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) In the nation's capital, [Andorra la Vella](/wiki/Andorra_la_Vella "Andorra la Vella"), temperatures were recorded to be over 36 °C (97 °F), far higher than the average for the time of year.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

### Croatia

The heat dome which caused extreme temperatures in north-west Europe was expected to affect Croatia from 21 to 24 July. Temperatures peaked on 23 July, reaching 38–39 °C (100–102 °F) in cities including [Zagreb](/wiki/Zagreb "Zagreb"), [Osijek](/wiki/Osijek "Osijek"), [Karlovac](/wiki/Karlovac "Karlovac"), [Slavonski Brod](/wiki/Slavonski_Brod "Slavonski Brod"), [Knin](/wiki/Knin "Knin"). The highest temperature of 39.4 °C (102.9 °F) was recorded in [Valpovo](/wiki/Valpovo "Valpovo").[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) Temperatures exceeding 40 °C (104 °F) were also recorded, but only at unofficial weather stations. The heat was ended the same day by a cold front in [continental Croatia](/wiki/Continental_Croatia "Continental Croatia"), but higher temperatures continued in the southern part of the country, which had been suffering a drought since 2021.[[38]](#cite_note-cro_nacional-38)

### France

#### June heatwave

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Heatwave_in_western_Europe_ESA24321812.jpeg/250px-Heatwave_in_western_Europe_ESA24321812.jpeg)

On 16 June, [Météo-France](/wiki/M%C3%A9t%C3%A9o-France "Météo-France") activated its red alert in 12 [departments](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France") and its orange alert in another 25 because of the heatwave. The departments on red alert were mainly those located in the south-west, along the Atlantic coast and the south; the heat was generally less severe further north and east. The heatwave was the earliest in the year since records began and marked the fourth time that a [red heat alert](/wiki/France_heat_alert "France heat alert") had been issued since the protocol was activated after the [2003 heatwave](/wiki/2003_European_heat_wave "2003 European heat wave").[[41]](#cite_note-41)

On 17 June, the red alert was activated in 14 more departments, adding the [Hautes-Pyrénées](/wiki/Hautes-Pyr%C3%A9n%C3%A9es "Hautes-Pyrénées") and the [Pyrénées-Atlantiques](/wiki/Pyr%C3%A9n%C3%A9es-Atlantiques "Pyrénées-Atlantiques") to the 12 of the previous day. The orange alert was activated in 56 more departments.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

#### July heatwave

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Wildfires_in_southwest_France_ESA24375606.gif/250px-Wildfires_in_southwest_France_ESA24375606.gif)

An estimated total of more than 20,800 ha (51,000 acres) were burnt by wildfires in [Gironde](/wiki/Gironde "Gironde"), causing a total of near 37,000 people to be evacuated.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

On 20 July, a baby died in an overheated car in the Pyrénées-Atlantiques.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Authorities reported the deaths of two others in work-related accidents,[[45]](#cite_note-45) which was raised to four on 28 July, all likely attributed to the extreme heat.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/S%C3%A9cheresse_2022_Haut-Jura.jpg/250px-S%C3%A9cheresse_2022_Haut-Jura.jpg)

Temperatures exceeded 40 °C (104 °F) even in [Brittany](/wiki/Brittany_(administrative_region) "Brittany (administrative region)"), which is unprecedented. It reached 42.6 °C (108.7 °F) in [Biscarrosse](/wiki/Biscarrosse "Biscarrosse") ([Landes](/wiki/Landes_(department) "Landes (department)")), 42.4 °C (108.3 °F) in [Cazaux](/wiki/Cazaux_Air_Base "Cazaux Air Base") ([Gironde](/wiki/Gironde "Gironde")), 42.0 °C (107.6 °F) in [Nantes](/wiki/Nantes "Nantes") ([Loire-Atlantique](/wiki/Loire-Atlantique "Loire-Atlantique")), 41.5 °C (106.7 °F) in [La Roche-sur-Yon](/wiki/La_Roche-sur-Yon "La Roche-sur-Yon") ([Vendée](/wiki/Vend%C3%A9e "Vendée")), 40.3 °C (104.5 °F) in [Lanmeur](/wiki/Lanmeur "Lanmeur") ([Finistère](/wiki/Finist%C3%A8re "Finistère")) and 39.3 °C (102.7 °F) in [Brest](/wiki/Brest,_France "Brest, France") (Finistère).[[47]](#cite_note-47)

This July heatwave aggravated the drought that had been ongoing in the country since the start of the year, making July 2022 the driest July since records began.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)

From 14 to 20 June, Germany saw 1,636 probable heat-related deaths attributed to temperatures reaching 39.2 °C (102.6 °F).[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)

#### July heatwave

From 11 to 17 July, Germany saw an all-deaths excess death rate of 16% followed by 23% in the week from 18 to 24 July. This corresponds to 6,502 excess deaths.[[54]](#cite_note-54) On 20 July, temperatures in several states reached new records. Temperatures in [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg") reached 40.1 °C (104.2 °F), 40.0 °C (104.0 °F) in [Lower Saxony](/wiki/Lower_Saxony "Lower Saxony") and [Saxony-Anhalt](/wiki/Saxony-Anhalt "Saxony-Anhalt"), 39.6 °C (103.3 °F) in [Mecklenburg-Vorpommern](/wiki/Mecklenburg-Vorpommern "Mecklenburg-Vorpommern") and 39.1 °C (102.4 °F) in [Schleswig-Holstein](/wiki/Schleswig-Holstein "Schleswig-Holstein").[[55]](#cite_note-55)
The highest temperature recorded on 20 July was 40.3 °C (104.5 °F) in [Bad Mergentheim](/wiki/Bad_Mergentheim "Bad Mergentheim").[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57)

#### Deaths

The [Robert Koch Institute](/wiki/Robert_Koch_Institute "Robert Koch Institute") put the death toll of the heatwaves at over 4,500.[[13]](#cite_note-GermanyDeaths-13)

On 18 July, a temperature of 34.2 °C (93.6 °F) was recorded at [Guernsey Airport](/wiki/Guernsey_Airport "Guernsey Airport"), the record for July and just 0.1 °C lower than Guernsey's all time record, which was on 9 August 2003.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

### Hungary

#### July heatwave

On 22 July, a temperature of 38.5 °C (101.3 °F) was reported at [Újpest](/wiki/%C3%9Ajpest "Újpest") in [Budapest](/wiki/Budapest "Budapest"), which broke the maximum temperature record for that day in the city.[[61]](#cite_note-61) On 23 July, a temperature of 41.5 °C (106.7 °F) was recorded at [Kiskunfélegyháza](/wiki/Kiskunf%C3%A9legyh%C3%A1za "Kiskunfélegyháza") and [Hódmezővásárhely](/wiki/H%C3%B3dmez%C5%91v%C3%A1s%C3%A1rhely "Hódmezővásárhely"), which almost broke the all-time temperature record of 41.9 °C (107.4 °F) in Hungary.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

#### August heatwave

On 2 August, [Lake Velence](/wiki/Lake_Velence "Lake Velence") reached record low water levels of 58 cm (23 in) due to severe drought.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

On 3 August, the National Meteorological Service issued a third-level heat warning, with temperatures forecast to reach the low 30s and possibly exceeding 35 °C (95 °F) from 4 to 6 August.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

On 9 August, the National Directorate General for Water issued a press release, which stated that in the first 7 months of 2022, the amount of rainfall was 45 per cent below average, making it the driest year since 1901.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

On 16 August, the National Meteorological Service issued a third-level heat warning, with temperatures forecast to reach above 35 °C (95 °F) from 17 to 19 August.[[66]](#cite_note-66) On 17 August, a temperature of 37 °C (99 °F) was recorded at [Baja](/wiki/Baja,_Hungary "Baja, Hungary") and [Kübekháza](/wiki/K%C3%BCbekh%C3%A1za "Kübekháza"), breaking the Hungarian temperature record for that day.[[67]](#cite_note-67) Due to the heat warning, the [Hungarian State Railways](/wiki/Hungarian_State_Railways "Hungarian State Railways") and [Volánbusz](/wiki/Vol%C3%A1n "Volán") were distributing mineral water at major railway stations, rural railway stations and bus stations.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

### Iceland

On 15 August 2022 the temperature reached 30.1 °C (86.2 °F) in [Neskaupstadur](/wiki/Neskaupstadur "Neskaupstadur").[[69]](#cite_note-weatherspark.com-69)
It was Iceland's warmest day since 22 June 1939.

### Ireland

#### July heatwave

[Met Éireann](/wiki/Met_%C3%89ireann "Met Éireann") issued a high-temperature advisory on 13 July, with temperatures forecast to reach the high 20s and possibly exceeding 30 °C (86 °F) from 17 to 19 July.[[70]](#cite_note-70) Met Éireann subsequently issued a Status Yellow high-temperature warning for Ireland on 15 July, with "exceptionally" high temperatures possibly reaching 32 °C (90 °F).[[71]](#cite_note-71)

On 18 July, a temperature of 33.2 °C (91.8 °F) was reported at [Allenwood](/wiki/Allenwood,_County_Kildare "Allenwood, County Kildare") one of the highest temperatures ever recorded in Ireland, breaking the Irish temperature record for July.[[72]](#cite_note-auto1-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73) The record highest temperature had been 33.3 °C (91.9 °F) at [Kilkenny Castle](/wiki/Kilkenny_Castle "Kilkenny Castle") in June 1887, but some in recent years have called for the reassessment of the previous record.[[72]](#cite_note-auto1-72)

#### August heatwave

[Met Éireann](/wiki/Met_%C3%89ireann "Met Éireann") issued a high-temperature advisory on 7 August, with temperatures forecast to reach above 25 °C (77 °F) for a period of five days or more from 10 to 14 August.[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75) Met Éireann subsequently issued a Status Yellow high-temperature warning for [Leinster](/wiki/Leinster "Leinster") and [Munster](/wiki/Munster "Munster") on 9 August, warning of "very warm or hot" from 11 to 13 August, with "maximum temperatures of 27–29 °C (81–84 °F)".[[76]](#cite_note-76) Met Éireann extended its high-temperature warning nationwide from 12 August with highs of 30 °C (86 °F) forecast.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

On 12 August, a temperature of 31.7 °C (89.1 °F) was reported at [Oak Park, County Carlow](/wiki/Oak_Park,_County_Carlow "Oak Park, County Carlow"), breaking the Irish temperature record for August.[[78]](#cite_note-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79)

Met Éireann issued a nationwide Status Orange thunderstorm warning on 14 August, with heavy downpours of rain and hail forecast.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

On 15 August the forecasted thunderstorms caused flooding, mainly in [County Roscommon](/wiki/County_Roscommon "County Roscommon") and [County Carlow](/wiki/County_Carlow "County Carlow").[[81]](#cite_note-81)

[Irish Water](/wiki/Irish_Water "Irish Water") appealed to people to conserve water as much as possible and warned that 37 water supplies around the country were being impacted by [drought](/wiki/Drought "Drought") conditions.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

### Italy

#### June to August heatwave

In Italy, the number of wildfires was three times the historical average by the end of June.[[83]](#cite_note-83) Temperatures in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") reached 40.8 °C (105.4 °F) on 28 June.[[84]](#cite_note-WashingtonPostEuropeHeatwave-84) On 22 July, sixteen cities including [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") were put on the red state of alert, the country's highest heatwave alert to warn of serious health risks.[[85]](#cite_note-Ansa-85) A [glacier collapse](/wiki/2022_Marmolada_serac_collapse "2022 Marmolada serac collapse") on the mountain of [Marmolada](/wiki/Marmolada "Marmolada") on 4 July killed eleven, and was attributed to the abnormally warm temperatures.[[86]](#cite_note-86) On 5 July, a state of emergency was declared in five northern regions in response to a severe drought in the [Po valley](/wiki/Po_Valley "Po Valley"), the worst in 70 years, and later for Tuscany.[[87]](#cite_note-87) In total, an estimated 18,010 excess deaths were reported due to the heat wave.[[14]](#cite_note-RAINEWS-14)

On the evening of 18 July, a large fire began in [Massarosa](/wiki/Massarosa "Massarosa"), [Lucca](/wiki/Lucca "Lucca"), which has destroyed 900 ha (2,200 acres) as of 21 July 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=2022_European_heatwaves&action=edit), reaching the province of [Pisa](/wiki/Pisa "Pisa").[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*][[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89)

On 19 July in [Trieste](/wiki/Trieste "Trieste"), there was a blackout caused by a fire in [Carso](/wiki/Carso "Carso").[[90]](#cite_note-90)

#### October heatwave

From 3 October a new heat wave (first [high pressure from the Azores](/wiki/Azores_High "Azores High"), and then an [African anticyclone](/w/index.php?title=African_anticyclone&action=edit&redlink=1 "African anticyclone (page does not exist)")) hits Italy, bringing [drought](/wiki/2022_European_drought "2022 European drought") back to northern [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy")[[91]](#cite_note-91) despite another 4 previous months of absent rains. 30 °C is exceeded in many locations.[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-93)

On 18 June, the temperature reached 33.8 °C (92.8 °F) in [Luxembourg City](/wiki/Luxembourg_City "Luxembourg City").[[94]](#cite_note-:9-94)

On 19 July, it reached 36.3 °C (97.3 °F) in Luxembourg City.[[94]](#cite_note-:9-94) And 40.1 °C (104.2 °F) in [Esch-sur-Alzette](/wiki/Esch-sur-Alzette "Esch-sur-Alzette").[[95]](#cite_note-95)

June was record warm for Malta, and the temperature reached a record high for June with 39.0 °C (102.2 °F) on 28 June.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

### Netherlands

On 18 July, the [Royal Netherlands Meteorological Institute](/wiki/Royal_Netherlands_Meteorological_Institute "Royal Netherlands Meteorological Institute") issued a code orange heat warning for the central and southern provinces, with forecasted temperatures of 36 °C (97 °F) in central provinces and 38–39 °C (100–102 °F) in the south on 19 July.[[98]](#cite_note-:7-98) On 19 July the temperature in [Maastricht](/wiki/Maastricht "Maastricht") reached 39.5 °C (103.1 °F).[[99]](#cite_note-99)

Extreme heat is rare in the Netherlands; there have been only nine days with temperatures above 35 °C (95 °F) since the start of measurement at the central weather station in 1901 (as of 18 July 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=2022_European_heatwaves&action=edit)).[[100]](#cite_note-100) In [2019, the Netherlands](/wiki/2019_European_heat_waves#Netherlands "2019 European heat waves") experienced temperatures surpassing 40 °C (104 °F) for the first time in recorded history, with 40.7 °C (105.3 °F) recorded in [Gilze-Rijen](/wiki/Gilze-Rijen_Air_Base "Gilze-Rijen Air Base").[[101]](#cite_note-101)

According to satellite measurements, the southernmost [Belgium–Netherlands border](/wiki/Belgium%E2%80%93Netherlands_border "Belgium–Netherlands border") may have reached 42 °C (108 °F), which could be the highest provisional temperature recorded in the country.[[102]](#cite_note-102)

# Heat-related mortality in Europe during the summer of 2022

[*Nature Medicine*](/nm)
**volume 29**, pages 1857–1866 (2023)[Cite this article](#citeas)

173k Accesses

501 Citations

7878 Altmetric

### Subjects

This article has been [updated](#change-history)

## Abstract

Over 70,000 excess deaths occurred in Europe during the summer of 2003. The resulting societal awareness led to the design and implementation of adaptation strategies to protect at-risk populations. We aimed to quantify heat-related mortality burden during the summer of 2022, the hottest season on record in Europe. We analyzed the Eurostat mortality database, which includes 45,184,044 counts of death from 823 contiguous regions in 35 European countries, representing the whole population of over 543 million people. We estimated 61,672 (95% confidence interval (CI) = 37,643–86,807) heat-related deaths in Europe between 30 May and 4 September 2022. Italy (18,010 deaths; 95% CI = 13,793–22,225), Spain (11,324; 95% CI = 7,908–14,880) and Germany (8,173; 95% CI = 5,374–11,018) had the highest summer heat-related mortality numbers, while Italy (295 deaths per million, 95% CI = 226–364), Greece (280, 95% CI = 201–355), Spain (237, 95% CI = 166–312) and Portugal (211, 95% CI = 162–255) had the highest heat-related mortality rates. Relative to population, we estimated 56% more heat-related deaths in women than men, with higher rates in men aged 0–64 (+41%) and 65–79 (+14%) years, and in women aged 80+ years (+27%). Our results call for a reevaluation and strengthening of existing heat surveillance platforms, prevention plans and long-term adaptation strategies.

The aim of this study was to use epidemiological models to estimate the sex- and age-specific mortality burden associated with the record-breaking temperatures registered during the 14-week period between 30 May and 4 September 2022 (weeks 22–35). Moreover, we compared this mortality burden within the broader context of the summer of 2003 and the accelerated warming observed in the continent during the last decade (2013–2022).

## Results

### Association between temperature and mortality

The cumulative temperature–mortality association in Europe shows a monotonically increasing risk of death for temperatures above and below the minimum mortality temperature (Fig. [1a,b](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig1); associations by sex and age groups available in Extended Data Fig. [1a–d](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig5)). This optimum temperature was around 17–19 °C, with small differences according to sex (18.32 °C for women and 18.55 °C for men), but generally warmer values for older adults (17.39, 18.33 and 18.56 °C for the age groups 0–64, 65–79 and 80+ years, respectively). The slopes of the relative risk (RR) association for temperatures colder and hotter than the optimum temperature also increased with age (Fig. [1b](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig1)); however, the age risk pattern differed according to sex (Extended Data Fig. [1b–d](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig5)). On the one hand, the slope for colder temperatures was similar in women and men, except in the age group 0–64 years, in which the risk was higher in men. On the other hand, the slope for hotter temperatures was steeper in men aged 0–64 years and in women aged 65–79 and 80+ years. The overall spatial distribution of the RR at the temperature 95th centile emphasizes the latitudinal differences in heat-related mortality risk (Fig. [1c](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig1)). Thus, the highest risks of heat-related mortality were observed in countries near the Mediterranean Sea in all sex and age groups, with generally higher values for older adults (Fig. [1d](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig1)) and women (Fig. [1e,f](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig1)).

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41591-023-02419-z/MediaObjects/41591_2023_2419_Fig1_HTML.png)

**a**,**b**, Cumulative relative risk of death (unitless) in Europe for the overall population (black), women (red) and men (blue) (**a**) and people aged 0–64 (blue), 65–79 (red) and 80+ (black) years (**b**), together with their 95% CIs (shadings). **c**–**f**, Regional relative risk of death (unitless) at the temperature 95th centile for the overall population (**c**), people aged 80+ years (**d**), women (**e**) and men (**f**).

### Temperatures and heat-related mortality numbers

Observed European mean temperatures uninterruptedly exceeded the baseline climatological values of the 1991–2020 period in all of the weeks during summer 2022 (Fig. [2a](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig2)). European weekly temperature anomalies in 2022 ranged between +0.78 and +2.33 °C in June (weeks 22–26), between +0.18 and +3.56 °C in July (weeks 26–30), and between +0.91 and +2.67 °C in August (weeks 31–35). Although several subcontinental warmer-than-average weeks were recorded during the summer (Extended Data Figs. [2](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig6)–[6](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig10)), the most intense, pan-European heat wave was observed during week 29 (between 18 and 24 July); this week alone was estimated to be associated with 11,637 (95% confidence interval (CI) = 7,639–15,970) heat-related deaths (Fig. [2b](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig2)), particularly in Central and Southern Europe (Extended Data Fig. [4c,d](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig8)). The largest temperature anomalies coincided with the peak of the mean annual cycle of temperatures, that is, from mid-July to mid-August (Fig. [2a](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig2)). The joint effect of the annual cycle and the anomalies magnified the mortality numbers and was associated with 38,881 (95% CI = 25,051–53,699) heat-related deaths between 11 July and 14 August (Fig. [2b](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig2)). Heat-related mortality during this 5-week period accounted for nearly two-thirds of the overall summer (61,672; 95% CI = 37,643–86,807; Table [1](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Tab1)) and annual (62,862; 95% CI = 37,935–88,780; Supplementary Table [1](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#MOESM1)) heat-related mortality numbers. Italy (18,010; 95% CI = 13,793–22,225), Spain (11,324; 95% CI = 7,908–14,880), Germany (8,173; 95% CI = 5,374–11,018), France (4,807; 95% CI = 1,739–8,123), the United Kingdom (3,469; 95% CI = 370–6,676) and Greece (3,092; 95% CI = 2,217–3,915) were the countries with the highest summer heat-related deaths (Table [1](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Tab1)).

![figure 2](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41591-023-02419-z/MediaObjects/41591_2023_2419_Fig2_HTML.png)

**a**, Weekly baseline (gray line) and observed (black line) temperature (°C) averaged over Europe. Temperature anomalies are defined as the difference between observed and baseline temperatures (gray shading). Baseline temperatures were computed as the mean annual cycle of observed temperatures in the reference period 1991–2020. **b**,**c**, Weekly heat-related mortality (weekly deaths) aggregated over Europe for the overall population (black), women (red) and men (blue) (**b**) and people aged 0–64 (blue), 65–79 (red) and 80+ (black) years (**c**), together with their 95% CIs (shadings). The numbers for women and men in **b** do not include the United Kingdom; values for the age groups in **c** do not include Germany, Ireland and the United Kingdom.

We estimated 63% more heat-related deaths in women (35,406; 95% CI = 21,576–46,634) than in men (21,667; 95% CI = 14,684–27,998) during the summer of 2022 (Table [1](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Tab1) and Supplementary Table [2](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#MOESM1)). The death toll steeply increased with age, with 4,822 (95% CI = 1,130–8,158), 9,226 (95% CI = 665–17,382) and 36,848 (95% CI = 27,591–45,509) heat-related deaths in the age groups 0–64, 65–79 and 80+ years, respectively (Table [2](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Tab2)). Italy, Spain, Germany and France had the highest female and male heat-related mortality numbers (Table [1](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Tab1)). Italy had the highest number of heat-related deaths among the age groups 65–79 and 80+ years, but France had the highest number of heat-related deaths among people aged 0–64 years (Table [2](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Tab2)). Despite differences in the magnitude of overall summer mortality numbers among different sex and age groups, the weekly changes in heat-related deaths were generally the same in all of them (Fig. [2b,c](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig2); weekly changes according to sex and age groups are available in Extended Data Fig. [1e–h](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig5)). The differences according to sex largely varied with age, with higher heat-related deaths in men aged 0–64 years (+43%), but in women aged 65–79 (+6%) and 80+ (+121%) years (Supplementary Table [2](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#MOESM1)).

### Temperatures and heat-related mortality rates

Recorded summer mean temperatures exceeded the baseline climatological values in all European countries with the only exception being Iceland (−0.43 °C; Fig. [3a](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig3)). The warmest summer temperature anomalies were mainly registered in Southwestern Europe, with the highest national values in France (+2.43 °C), Switzerland (+2.30 °C), Italy (+2.28 °C), Hungary (+2.13 °C) and Spain (+2.11 °C). The highest summer heat-related mortality rates were found in countries near the Mediterranean Sea, that is, Italy (295 deaths per million, 95% CI = 226–364), Greece (280, 95% CI = 201–355), Spain (237, 95% CI = 166–312) and Portugal (211, 95% CI = 162–255; Table [1](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Tab1)), as well as in specific regions in Bulgaria, Romania, Croatia and Southern France (Fig. [3b](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig3)). Overall, we estimated 114 (95% CI = 69–160) heat-related deaths per million in Europe during the summer, with 145 (95% CI = 89–192) female and 93 (95% CI = 63–120) male deaths per million (Table [1](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Tab1)). The heat-related mortality rate also steeply increased with age, with 16 (95% CI = 4–27), 160 (95% CI = 12–302) and 1,684 (95% CI = 1,261–2,080) deaths per million in the age groups 0–64, 65–79 and 80+ years, respectively (Table [2](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Tab2)). The differences according to sex largely varied with age, with higher heat-related mortality rates in men aged 0–64 (+41%) and 65–79 (+14%) years, and in women aged 80+ years (+27%) and for all age groups combined (+56%; Supplementary Table [2](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#MOESM1)). These age-dependent sex differences were found in most countries with, for example, higher heat-related mortality rates in men than in women for the age group 65–79 years (Fig. [3c,d](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig3)), but higher in women than in men for people aged 80+ years (Fig. [3e,f](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig3)).

![figure 3](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41591-023-02419-z/MediaObjects/41591_2023_2419_Fig3_HTML.png)

**a**, Regional temperature anomaly (°C) averaged over the summer. **b**–**f**, Regional heat-related mortality rate (summer deaths per million) aggregated over the summer for the whole population (**b**), women aged 65–79 years (**c**), men aged 65–79 years (**d**), women aged 80+ years (**e**) and men aged 80+ years (**f**). Summer refers to the 14-week period between 30 May and 4 September 2022 (weeks 22–35).

### Climate change and the summer of 2022

On average over the 35 European countries analyzed here, the summer of 2022 was the warmest season on record (20.30 °C = *µ* + 2.51 *σ*), which exceeded both the summer of 2003 (20.20 °C = *µ* + 2.36 *σ*) and the threshold of 2.5 s.d. (*σ* = 0.67 °C) over the mean (*µ* = 18.62 °C) of the distribution of summer mean temperatures during the 1991–2020 period. The summer of 2003 was an exceptionally warm season within a period of relatively constant global and continental temperatures, commonly referred to as the global warming hiatus of 1998–2012 (ref. [27](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#ref-CR27 "Medhaug, I., Stolpe, M. B., Fischer, E. M. & Knutti, R. Reconciling controversies about the ‘global warming hiatus’. Nature 545, 41–47 (2017).")) (Fig. [4a](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig4)). During the last decade (2013–2022), however, summer mean temperatures in the analyzed European countries sped up at an approximately constant rate of +0.142 °C per year, compared with the modest rate of +0.028 °C per year in 1991–2012 (the slopes in Fig. [4a](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig4)). In that regard, although summer mean temperatures in 2022 followed the trend observed in the last decade (Fig. [4a](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig4)), this was associated with an increase in 25,561 summer heat-related deaths compared to 2015–2021 (Fig. [4b](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig4)). In this regard, we estimated that the warming observed since 2015 was associated with 18,547 additional summer heat-related deaths for every +1 °C increase in temperature (the slope in Fig. [4b](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig4)), or in relative terms, 35.3 additional summer heat-related deaths per million for every +1 °C increase in temperature. Moreover, in the absence of adaptation to future summer warming, and by forward extrapolating the linear fittings in Fig. [4a,b](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig4), we would expect a heat-related mortality burden of 68,116 deaths on average every summer by the year 2030, 94,363 deaths by 2040 and 120,610 deaths by 2050.

![figure 4](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41591-023-02419-z/MediaObjects/41591_2023_2419_Fig4_HTML.png)

**a**, Year-to-year time series of summer mean temperatures (°C) averaged over the analyzed European countries. The straight lines depict the linear fitting for the 1991–2012 (excluding the year 2003) and 2013–2022 periods. **b**, Relationship between summer mean temperature (°C) and summer heat-related mortality (summer deaths) in the analyzed European countries. The straight line shows the linear fitting for the 2015–2022 period.

### Sensitivity analyses

We performed sensitivity analyses by modifying the modeling choices ([Methods](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Sec9)). In the main results, we calibrated the epidemiological models with temperature and mortality data from January 2015 to December 2019 to avoid any eventual interfering effect of the coronavirus disease 2019 pandemic. However, sensitivity analyses showed that estimates of the RRs and heat-related mortality during the summer (68,476 deaths; 95% CI = 44,884–90,803) and year (70,997; 95% CI = 46,612–94,777) of 2022 were only slightly higher when the pandemic period was included in the calibration of the epidemiological models, that is, 2015–2022 (Supplementary Table [1](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#MOESM1) and Extended Data Fig. [7](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig11)).

The statistical analysis was done in two steps ([Methods](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Sec9)). In the first stage, we used quasi-Poisson regression models to calculate the location-specific temperature–lag–mortality relationship in each European region. We tested different configurations of the exposure–response function, being estimates of the heat-related mortality not sensitive to these choices (Supplementary Table [1](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#MOESM1)). In the main results, we used the model configuration that better fitted the data based on the Akaike information criterion. In the second stage, we used a multivariate, multilevel meta-regression analysis to pool the location-specific coefficients obtained in the first step, including (1) country random effects and (2) the location-specific temperature average, (3) the temperature interquartile range and (4) the percentage of people aged 80+ years as meta-predictors (Extended Data Fig. [8](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#Fig12)). We tested these meta-predictors and we found that they explained a significant fraction of the spatial heterogeneity (Supplementary Table [3](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#MOESM1)).

## Discussion

This European-wide study quantified the mortality associated with record-breaking temperatures during the summer of 2022, currently the hottest season on record in Europe, and analyzed it within the broader context of the summer of 2003 and the accelerated warming observed in the continent during the last decade (2013–2022). Epidemiological models were applied to a mortality database representing the whole population of over 543 million people from 823 contiguous regions in 35 European countries. Overall, we estimated 62,862 heat-related deaths in Europe in 2022; 61,672 of those deaths occurred between 30 May and 4 September. Italy, Spain, Germany, France, the United Kingdom and Greece had the highest summer heat-related mortality numbers. In relative terms, the largest summer heat-related mortality rates were found in countries near the Mediterranean Sea, which included Italy, Greece, Spain and Portugal. The results showed that there was a large increase in heat-related mortality during June–August 2022, approaching the record-breaking excess mortality of June–September 2003.

However, the comparison with 2003 needs to be interpreted with caution, given the large methodological differences in our study compared to previous estimates[12](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#ref-CR12 "Robine, J.-M. et al. Death toll exceeded 70,000 in Europe during the summer of 2003. C. R. Biol. 331, 171–178 (2008)."). First, the estimate of the death toll during the summer of 2003 from Robine et al.[12](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#ref-CR12 "Robine, J.-M. et al. Death toll exceeded 70,000 in Europe during the summer of 2003. C. R. Biol. 331, 171–178 (2008).") was based on excess mortality numbers, which quantify the deviation of the mortality from the seasonally varying expected mortality from a reference period. As heat-related mortality occurs every summer, the estimate of excess deaths may exclude part of the heat-related mortality burden. On the other hand, this study used epidemiological models to quantify deaths specifically attributable to heat; therefore, our estimate is not expected to include mortality cases in which heat was not a contributor. Furthermore, we used weekly temperature and mortality data in our epidemiological models, which is expected to underestimate the day-to-day variability of the time series and possibly their lagged short-term associations. This is especially the case for heat, given that the risk of death is acute and generally does not last for more than 5 days[7](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#ref-CR7 "Martínez-Solanas, È. et al. Projections of temperature attributable mortality in Europe: a timeseries analysis in 147 contiguous regions. Lancet Planet. Health 5, e446–e454 (2021)."),[28](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#ref-CR28 "Achebak, H., Devolder, D. & Ballester, J. Trends in temperature-related age-specific and sex-specific mortality from cardiovascular diseases in Spain: a national time-series analysis. Lancet Planet. Health 3, e297–e306 (2019)."),[29](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#ref-CR29 "Achebak, H., Devolder, D., Ingole, V. & Ballester, J. Reversal of the seasonality of temperature-attributable mortality from respiratory diseases in Spain. Nat. Commun. 11, 2457 (2020)."). As a reference, a previous study[30](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#ref-CR30 "Tobias, A., Royé, D. & Íñiguez, C. Heat-attributable mortality in the summer of 2022 in Spain. Epidemiology 34, e5–e6 (2023).") applied similar epidemiological models to daily temperature and mortality data for Spain only, and found that the summer heat-related mortality burden was 6% higher (that is, 12,054 deaths) than the one reported here for the same country (that is, 11,324 deaths). Finally, the excess mortality estimates during the summer of 2003 from Robine et al.[12](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#ref-CR12 "Robine, J.-M. et al. Death toll exceeded 70,000 in Europe during the summer of 2003. C. R. Biol. 331, 171–178 (2008).") were based on data from only 16 European countries, representing a population of nearly 400 million people[31](/articles/s41591-023-02419-z#ref-CR31 "Ball

enriched_points.point_title: Increased likelihood due to climate change
enriched_points.expanded_content: The 2022 European heatwave was significantly intensified by climate change, with scientists attributing over half of the heat-related deaths to anthropogenic warming.
Heatwaves in Europe are now estimated to be 30 times more likely due to climate change, highlighting the urgent need for climate action.
The heatwave caused approximately 70,000 excess deaths and severely impacted agriculture, leading to crop failures and economic losses across multiple European countries.
